items.q: What defines epic poetry as a genre?
items.correct: Long narrative poems on heroic subjects, in elevated style, often invoking a muse, in medias res openings, and treating events of national or cosmic significance.
items.misconception: Epic means any poem that is very long.
items.random_wrong: Epic means any story with a battle in it.
items.why: Length alone isn't enough. Epic has specific conventions (invocation, elevated diction, heroic subject) inherited from Homer through Virgil to Milton — students reduce it to scale.

items.correct: A line of five iambs (unstressed-stressed syllable pairs), creating a ten-syllable line with the rhythm da-DUM da-DUM da-DUM da-DUM da-DUM.
items.misconception: Iambic pentameter is any line with ten syllables.
items.random_wrong: Iambic pentameter is when every line rhymes.
items.why: Syllable count alone doesn't make iambic pentameter — the stress pattern is essential. And iambic pentameter is usually unrhymed (blank verse) in Shakespeare's plays.

items.q: Why are genre conventions important when interpreting a text?
items.correct: They set reader expectations the text can fulfil or subvert; analysing how a text uses or breaks conventions reveals its meaning and significance.
items.misconception: Genre is just a label to help bookshops categorise novels.
items.random_wrong: Genre means whether a book is fiction or non-fiction.
items.why: Genre is interpretively active. A horror novel that subverts horror conventions does so meaningfully — but only if you know the conventions. Students treat genre as labelling, not as a reading lens.

items.q: A scene after the main story ends is an.
items.why: It's Epilogue. “Prologue” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A smaller storyline beside the main plot is a.
items.why: It's Subplot. “Scene” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many classic conflict types (vs character, self, society, nature)?
items.why: Exact answer: 4. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
